questions.question: 15-year-old mentor says they're transgender, asks you not to tell unsupportive parents, mentions 'things would be easier if I wasn't here.' What do you do?

options.text: Promise secret, wait to see if they bring up suicidal thoughts again
Ask directly about suicide. If yes, assess urgency, call Trevor Project/988, involve supportive adults. Honor identity while ensuring safety.
Tell them being transgender is phase, they'll feel better accepting biological sex
Immediately tell parents despite request not to

questions.explanation: 'Easier if I wasn't here' = suicide warning sign. Ask directly: 'Are you thinking about killing yourself?' If yes, call Trevor Project (LGBTQ+ crisis: 1-866-488-7386, text START to 678-678) or 988. Don't out to unsupportive parents BUT involve supportive adult (school counselor, affirming family). ONE accepting adult reduces suicide risk 40%. Never promise secrecy when safety at risk. 'Phase/biological sex' language is conversion therapy (harmful, increases suicide risk).

questions.question: What are the confidentiality limits when working with minors in MHFA?

options.text: Absolute confidentiality — never break privacy with youth
Offer privacy but NOT absolute confidentiality; safety (suicide, abuse, immediate danger) overrides privacy; be transparent upfront about limits
Tell parents everything the youth shares
Minors have no privacy rights

questions.explanation: Confidentiality with minors: offer privacy BUT safety overrides. Must break confidence if: suicidal thoughts with plan/intent, abuse/neglect (mandatory reporting), immediate danger. Be transparent UPFRONT: 'What you tell me is private UNLESS I'm worried about your safety or someone is hurting you — then I need to get help.' Don't promise absolute confidentiality (can't keep). Don't tell parents everything (breaks trust). Privacy matters but safety comes first.

questions.question: Why do LGBTQ+ youth face higher mental health risks, and what is the most protective factor?

options.text: Being LGBTQ+ itself causes mental illness; no protective factors exist
Minority stress (discrimination, family rejection, bullying) causes 2-3x higher depression/anxiety, 4x higher suicide attempts; ONE accepting adult reduces suicide risk 40%
LGBTQ+ youth face same risks as heterosexual youth
Only therapy can reduce LGBTQ+ youth mental health risks

questions.explanation: LGBTQ+ youth: 2-3x higher depression/anxiety, 4x higher suicide attempts, 40% trans youth attempted suicide. Root cause: minority stress (discrimination, family rejection, bullying) NOT sexual orientation/gender identity itself. MOST PROTECTIVE FACTOR: ONE accepting adult reduces suicide risk 40%. Your acceptance MATTERS. Conversion therapy is harmful/discredited. Affirming support is evidence-based protective factor.

questions.question: When do you have a mandatory reporting obligation for child abuse, and what should you do?

options.text: No mandatory reporting obligations for MHFA responders
Report if suspect: physical/sexual abuse, neglect, emotional abuse. Call CPS/state hotline. Tell child it's not their fault. Do NOT confront alleged abuser or investigate yourself.
Only report if you witness abuse directly
Confront the alleged abuser first to get their side of story

questions.explanation: Mandatory reporting applies if you SUSPECT (not just witness): physical/sexual abuse, neglect, emotional abuse by parent/caregiver. CALL Child Protective Services (CPS) or state hotline (national: 1-800-422-4453). Tell child: 'You were right to tell me. Not your fault. I'm getting you help.' Do NOT confront alleged abuser (dangerous, can escalate). Do NOT investigate (that's CPS role). Your role: bridge to protection. Laws vary by state/role, but err on side of reporting when child safety at risk.

questions.question: According to research, when does most lifetime mental illness begin?

options.text: Most mental illness begins in late adulthood (60+)
Half of all lifetime mental illness begins by age 14, three-quarters by age 24
Mental illness onset is evenly distributed across lifespan
Childhood mental illness always resolves by adulthood

questions.explanation: Research: HALF of all lifetime mental illness begins by age 14, THREE-QUARTERS by age 24. This highlights critical importance of early identification and intervention in youth. Adolescent brain development (prefrontal cortex not fully mature until ~25) affects risk-taking, emotion regulation. Early treatment improves long-term outcomes. Youth need specialized MHFA approaches respecting developmental stage.

title: Supporting Youth and Adolescent Mental Health - Quiz
